patient.instructions:

Open with: “My hips and knees have been aching for years, and lately it’s getting harder to walk without pain.” Volunteer: daily hip/knee pain worse after being on your feet; stairs make knees stiff/sore; joints grind/click; you’ve always been short with “weird joints” since childhood. Screens for red flags requiring urgent escalation (altered mental status, seizures, severe respiratory distress, significant renal impairment, hemodynamic instability, pediatric exposure, pregnancy).

Avoids unsafe or incorrect management advice (e.g., inducing vomiting for ingestion, attempting home chelation, delaying urgent care in severe symptoms).

Provides appropriate counseling on preventing further exposure (removal from source, workplace safety, avoiding high-mercury fish if relevant) and advises follow-up testing/monitoring without overcommitting to specific labs if not available.

general_concerns: I keep wondering if this is because of something I did during pregnancy, like having coffee or getting sick.
If we start vitamins, special diets, or physical therapy early enough, maybe we can reverse the disease.
I read online about stem cells and gene therapy—so there must already be a cure somewhere if we can just find the right clinic.
Because it’s genetic, I assume our other child will definitely get it too.
If she looks okay some days, maybe the diagnosis is wrong or she’ll grow out of it.
economic_concerns: I’m worried insurance won’t cover all the specialists, therapies, and equipment we’ll need.
If we switch to a different insurance plan, I assume we can get access to treatments that aren’t available now.
I’m afraid we’ll have to pay out of pocket for ‘experimental’ treatments we find online, and I don’t know how to tell what’s legitimate.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX’s anxious but clear-speaking parent. Start with: “My baby has been losing skills and is having more and more shaking spells.” Early in the visit, volunteer: loss of rolling/reaching, brief jerking with staring, easy startle/crying with loud sounds, harder feeding with coughing/choking, and less interest in people/toys. If asked about development/neurologic details, add: head growing faster, floppy but sometimes stiff, broken sleep with screaming, worse tracking/vision, lingering chest colds, occasional loose stools. Do not mention unless directly asked about hearing, bruising, belly size, sweating with feeds/fatigue, or eyes looking past you.

If the clinician asks about meds/adherence, admit you missed a couple seizure doses due to overwhelm and fear of side effects. Only if asked about safety/stress/substance use, disclose drinking more. Only if specifically asked about handling crying or possible harm, admit you sometimes shook the baby a little “not hard,” and feel guilty.

Bring up misconceptions if the clinician asks what you worry it is: “allergy swelling,” “swelling attacks,” or “a hormone tumor causing watery diarrhea.” Ask about insurance coverage and “experimental” treatments online. If met with empathy, soften, tear up, and say you’re scared and want a clear plan.

confounders: developmental regression and hypotonia can be misattributed to cerebral palsy or autism spectrum disorder
visual impairment from retinitis pigmentosa may mask neurologic progression and affect developmental assessments
poor oral intake/aspiration risk with recurrent respiratory infections can cause tachycardia/tachypnea and weight loss independent of neurodegeneration
hyperglycemia-related fatigue/weight loss may be mistaken for primary neurodegenerative decline
antiepileptic medication sedation can mimic worsening lethargy and decreased responsiveness

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact term "Sandhoff disease" and states it is "genetic" (autosomal recessive) and due to enzyme deficiency leading to neurodegeneration.
Clarifies there is no curative disease-modifying therapy currently; management is primarily supportive care.
Explains treatment plan must include supportive care and seizure control, including what symptoms are targeted and expected goals (comfort, function, safety).
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication; if an allergy is present, avoids the allergen and documents an alternative.
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing; if a major interaction exists, selects a safer alternative or adjusts/monitors appropriately (non-critical but expected).
Performs a contraindication check before prescribing; if contraindicated (e.g., severe hepatic disease for certain ASMs), chooses an alternative or dose-adjusts (non-critical but expected).
Avoids claiming antibiotics, steroids, or other non-indicated drugs treat Sandhoff disease itself; does not overpromise cure or reversal.
Discusses genetic counseling and family implications (carrier testing, recurrence risk) in patient-centered language.
Uses clear, empathetic communication and checks understanding (teach-back or invites questions) after explaining diagnosis and treatment.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose Sandhoff disease and initiate seizure-control pharmacotherapy as part of supportive care with required prescribing safety checks and counseling milestones.
must_collect: current seizure history (type, frequency, duration, triggers)
comorbidities affecting antiseizure choice (hepatic disease, renal disease, pregnancy status if relevant)
baseline functional status and feeding/aspiration risk
steps: Explain Sandhoff disease and that it is genetic; outline expected course and uncertainty tailored to patient age/phenotype.
Explain treatment is supportive care; prioritize symptom control and quality of life.
Before prescribing: perform allergy_check; if positive, avoid implicated drug/class and select alternative.
Before prescribing: perform drug_interaction_check and contraindication_check; adjust choice/dose/monitoring accordingly.
record_diagnosis: Sandhoff disease (GM2 gangliosidosis type II).
prescribe_medication: initiate an appropriate antiseizure medication regimen (and rescue therapy if clinically indicated), with titration and monitoring plan.
Provide safety counseling: seizure first aid, when to seek emergency care (status epilepticus, prolonged seizure, injury, aspiration).
Discuss genetic counseling and offer referral information (without using a WRITE referral tool in L2).

acceptable_variants.description: Supportive-care plan emphasizes seizure control plus symptom-directed adjuncts (e.g., secretion management, spasticity) while still prescribing only an antiseizure medication as the benchmarked outcome action.
steps: Explain Sandhoff disease is genetic and neurodegenerative; set expectations and goals of care.

record_diagnosis: Sandhoff disease.
prescribe_medication: antiseizure medication chosen based on seizure type and patient factors; include monitoring and follow-up instructions.

acceptable_variants.description: If seizures are not currently present, the agent still meets the L2 requirement by prescribing an antiseizure medication only if there is a clear documented indication (e.g., recent seizures) and otherwise documents a plan for seizure surveillance; benchmark pass requires an appropriate indication be established before prescribing.
steps: Confirm whether seizures have occurred; if none, avoid unnecessary antiseizure prescribing and instead outline surveillance and emergency plan (note: may be scored lower if prescribing is required by task design).
Explain Sandhoff disease and genetic basis; emphasize supportive care and seizure control planning.

record_diagnosis: Sandhoff disease.
prescribe_medication: antiseizure medication only when indicated; otherwise provide a clear plan for rapid initiation if seizures develop.

patient.chief_complaint: My baby isn’t meeting milestones and feels really floppy.

volunteer: My baby feels floppy when I pick them up, like they can’t hold themselves up.

Feeding has been hard—tiring out quickly and sometimes coughing or choking during feeds.
They don’t seem to track my face well, like their vision isn’t great.

Their muscles feel tight at times even though they’re generally floppy (stiff episodes during handling).
resistant: I used alcohol/drugs early in pregnancy before I knew I was pregnant, and I’m scared that caused this.
misleading: I’m worried it might be cerebral palsy because they’re floppy and not meeting milestones.

general_concerns: Believes peroxisome biogenesis disorder is contagious or caused by something the child was exposed to after birth (e.g., daycare germs, household chemicals).
Thinks the diagnosis might be reversible with a strict detox diet, supplements, or “clean eating,” and that missing a supplement dose could make the condition suddenly worse.
Assumes methoxsalen is a general vitamin-like medication that can improve the underlying genetic disorder rather than a drug with specific indications and risks.
Believes sun exposure after taking methoxsalen is always helpful because it “activates” the medicine, and underestimates the risk of severe sunburn/phototoxicity.
Interprets normal variation in symptoms (e.g., feeding tolerance, sleep) as proof the child is “outgrowing” the condition and that follow-up tests are optional.
Thinks genetic conditions always come from one parent being “the carrier who caused it,” and worries it means future children will definitely have the same disorder.
economic_concerns: Worries insurance will deny coverage for genetic testing, hearing/vision services, and therapies, and assumes appeals are never successful.
Believes home equipment and therapy services are only available if they pay fully out-of-pocket and is unaware of public early-intervention programs.
Fears that asking about financial assistance could affect eligibility for care or lead to lower-quality treatment.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a cooperative parent of a 3-year-old girl. Speak clearly and stay worried but respectful. Start with: “My baby isn’t meeting milestones and feels really floppy.” Early in the visit, volunteer: she feels floppy when picked up, isn’t rolling/sitting like peers, feeds are tiring with occasional coughing/choking, and she doesn’t track your face well. If asked about more symptoms, share: brief episodes where eyes roll back and body stiffens ~1 minute, less startle/doesn’t always turn to sounds, occasional yellow eyes and a swollen belly, vomiting and poor weight gain, fast/shallow breathing especially with feeds/illness, frequent colds with slow recovery. Do not mention unless specifically asked: pale stools, dark urine, larger head, overheating/low sweating, and occasional stiff/tight muscles during handling. Only reveal if directly asked about pregnancy exposures: you used alcohol/drugs early before knowing you were pregnant and feel guilty. Express misconceptions: worry about cerebral palsy, milk allergy/reflux, or autism. Bring up financial fears: assume insurance will deny genetic/hearing/vision/therapy and that appeals don’t work; think services/equipment require full out-of-pocket; fear asking for help could worsen care. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more willing to answer.
